monthly.headline: December concentrates $71B — 48% more than the monthly average
monthly.subline: The year-end "rush": Oct–Dec accumulate 30% of annual spend in just 3 months
monthly.ariaLabel: Monthly federal spending dot matrix, Jan to Dec 2023, each dot 1.6B MXN
monthly.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2023 · Each dot = 1.6B MXN · RUBLI v0.8.5

ghostNetwork.headline: 42 vendors, 5 addresses — the geography of invoicing fraud
ghostNetwork.lede: Constellation of definitive EFOS vendors sharing a handful of fiscal addresses. Each node is a vendor; size = contract count; color = risk score v0.8.5.
ghostNetwork.ariaLabel: Network of 42 ghost vendors clustered around 5 shared addresses
ghostNetwork.findingBody: Definitive EFOS vendors share a structural signature: few contracts (typically 2–5), short lifespan (2–3 years), and shared fiscal addresses. The v0.8.5 model underdetects them (score 0.28) because it was trained on large cases (IMSS, SEGALMEX) — a systematic detection gap.
ghostNetwork.footer: Source: SAT Art. 69-B CFF · cross-referenced with COMPRANET 2018–2024 · 38 RFC confirmed + 125 by name

emptyGranary.headline: Four companies, MXN $14.8B, zero competition
emptyGranary.subline: Top vendors by total value · AMLO 2019–24
emptyGranary.ariaLabel: Top agriculture vendors dot matrix, each dot 100M MXN
emptyGranary.findingBody: ILAS Mexico and Productos Loneg received 100% of their contracts without bidding. Average per contract: MXN 275M and MXN 302M respectively.
emptyGranary.footer: Source: COMPRANET · Each dot = $100M MXN · RUBLI v0.8.5 analysis

daBySector.headline: Each row is a sector — each dot is 2 percentage points
daBySector.subline: All cross the OECD line. Agriculture reaches 93% · Other, the lowest, triples the limit.
daBySector.ariaLabel: Direct award rate by sector as dot strip, 12 sectors, OECD ceiling at 25%

daRateTrend.headline: Direct award rose from 63% to 82% across three presidential terms
daRateTrend.subline: 3.3× the OECD limit of 25% — every administration worse than the last
daRateTrend.ariaLabel: Direct award rate by year 2010 to 2024, colored by presidential term

covidEmergency.headline: COVID-19 didn't create the crisis — it accelerated it
covidEmergency.subline: Direct award rose during the pandemic but never came back down. Single-bid hit 19.1% in 2021.

amloEra.headline: AMLO averaged 79.4% — every administration tops the last
amloEra.subline: +15.9 pts vs Fox era. The 81.9% peak is 3.3× the OECD limit.
amloEra.ariaLabel: DA peak rate by sexenio, dot matrix, each dot 1pp
amloEra.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2002–2025 · Each dot = 1pp · RUBLI v0.8.5

sexenioComp.headline: Every indicator worsens with each presidential term
sexenioComp.ariaLabel: Four metrics by sexenio, dot matrix, each dot 1pp
sexenioComp.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2002–2025 · Each dot = 1pp · RUBLI v0.8.5

adminFingerprints.headline: Each president left a distinct corruption fingerprint in the data
adminFingerprints.lede: Five radar charts reveal how procurement practices shifted with each administration. The five dimensions — risk score, high-risk rate, direct award percentage, total value, and contract volume — create a unique “fingerprint” for each presidency.
adminFingerprints.stat1Label: Peña Nieto HR · lowest of all 5
adminFingerprints.findingBody: AMLO's administration increased direct awards to 79.5% — the highest in 23 years and 3.2× the OECD recommended maximum of 25%. Despite anti-corruption rhetoric, the data shows less competition, not more.
adminFingerprints.caveatBody: Sheinbaum data covers only 2025 (partial year). The 12.0% high-risk rate may normalize as more contracts are recorded. Fox-era data (2002–2006) has the lowest data quality (Structure A), making risk comparisons less reliable for that period.
adminFingerprints.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2002–2025 · 3.05M contracts · RUBLI v0.8.5 model · 5 administrations

adminSunburst.headline: MXN 9.9 trillion across 5 presidents — where did the money go?
adminSunburst.lede: Each administration left a distinct procurement fingerprint. Peña Nieto's administration was the largest spender at MXN 3.1T, while AMLO's government shifted spending toward health and social programs. The inner ring shows each president's share; the outer ring breaks it down by sector.
adminSunburst.readGuideInnerBody: each arc represents one presidential administration, sized by total procurement spending.
adminSunburst.readGuideOuterBody: each segment is one of 12 sectors, sized by that sector's spending under the corresponding president. Hover to see exact values.

riskCalendar.headline: Ten years of procurement risk — December never cools down
riskCalendar.lede: Each cell in this heatmap represents one month's average risk score across all federal contracts. The pattern is unmistakable: the rightmost column (December) is consistently the darkest shade. The 2020 row shows an exceptional horizontal streak — COVID emergency procurement pushed risk scores up across all months.
riskCalendar.covidBody: Emergency procurement bypassed normal competitive procedures. Average risk scores reached their highest sustained levels in the 10-year window.
riskCalendar.decemberBody: Year-end budget exhaustion is visible in every single row. December risk averages are 64% higher than the October trough — the most reliable seasonal signal in the data.
riskCalendar.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2016–2025 · monthly average risk scores · RUBLI v0.8.5

procedureBreakdown.headline: 9 of 12 sectors award more than half their contracts without competition
procedureBreakdown.lede: Each sector is a block with three bands: red = direct award, orange = single-bidder tender, green = open tender. Each dot is 2 percentage points.
procedureBreakdown.ariaLabel: Procedure breakdown dot matrix: 12 sectors, 3 strips each (direct award, single bid, open tender)
procedureBreakdown.findingBody: Defense has the highest single-bidder tender rate (22.1%) — nearly a quarter of its "competitive" tenders receive only one proposal. Combined with 56.3% direct award, effectively 78.4% of Defense contracts lack real competition.
procedureBreakdown.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2002–2025 · 3.05M contracts · OECD Public Procurement Report 2023

nuevosRicos.headline: Vendors created under AMLO are born more opaque — and richer
nuevosRicos.subline: Each point is one registration-year cohort · X = direct award · Y = value captured
nuevosRicos.ariaLabel: Scatter plot of vendor cohorts by registration year: direct award rate versus total value captured
nuevosRicos.findingBody: AMLO's centroid (orange node) sits up and to the right: more value captured with greater opacity. Cohorts move into high-risk territory (red) as the X axis advances.
nuevosRicos.footer: Source: COMPRANET · cohorts by year of first federal registration · RUBLI v0.8.5

austeridad.headline: Spending fell 10%. Opacity rose 8 points.
austeridad.subline: Total spend and direct-award rate by administration · COMPRANET 2007–2024
austeridad.ariaLabel: Spend vs DA rate by administration, dot matrix

hemoserSplitting.headline: Splitting isn't an accident: 93K suspicious contracts under AMLO
hemoserSplitting.footer: * 2024 partial year · Splitting = multiple same-day contracts to the same vendor · Source: RUBLI v0.8.5

ceroCompetencia.headline: Fewer than 1 in 10 Agriculture and Education contracts had real competition
ceroCompetencia.subline: % of contracts with competitive procedure by sector · AMLO 2019–2024
ceroCompetencia.ariaLabel: Competitive % by sector, dot matrix, OECD 75% target
ceroCompetencia.findingBody: No sector meets the OECD 75% competitive target. The 4 worst sectors (Agriculture, Education, Labor, Treasury) are 60+ points below the standard.
ceroCompetencia.footer: Source: COMPRANET · OECD target: 75% competitive · Each dot = 1pp · RUBLI v0.8.5

riskPyramid.headline: 6% of contracts hold 42% of the money — and they're all critical risk
riskPyramid.lede: Each dot represents 2% of total contracts. Tiers narrow upward: the critical apex is narrow by count, but widest by value.
riskPyramid.ariaLabel: Dot pyramid showing 4 risk levels, critical at the top with 6 percent, low at the base with 59.4 percent
riskPyramid.summaryLine: HR = 13.49% · RUBLI v0.8.5 model · test AUC 0.828
riskPyramid.findingPrefix: The pyramid inverts when the measure shifts from count to value:
riskPyramid.findingStrong: 6% of contracts carry 42% of the money
riskPyramid.findingSuffix: . The pattern holds across all 12 sectors — corruption gravitates toward large contracts.
riskPyramid.footer: Source: 3.05M contracts · RUBLI v0.8.5 · thresholds critical≥0.60, high≥0.40, medium≥0.25

avalanchaDiciembre.headline: 51.4 billion in 31 days — the month the budget flees the calendar
avalanchaDiciembre.lede: Federal monthly spending in 2014 · each dot represents one billion pesos. December tripled the monthly average because the Budget Law forces unspent funds to return to the Treasury before the 31st.
avalanchaDiciembre.ariaLabel: Monthly federal spending 2014: January through November baseline, December explodes to 51.4B MXN
avalanchaDiciembre.findingBody: December concentrates 2.5–4× the monthly average spending across the last 23 years — a bipartisan pattern. The urgency is accounting-driven, not operational: deliveries are scheduled for February or March of the following year.
avalanchaDiciembre.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2014 · 7,215 contracts · Federal Budget and Treasury Responsibility Law

casaContratos.kicker: RUBLI · Institutional capture · Grupo Higa network
casaContratos.headline: The contract house: one institution, seven vendors, 85 billion
casaContratos.lede: Each satellite is a company linked to Juan Armando Hinojosa Cantú. Size reflects contract count; color, statistical risk. The capture pattern is visible: a closed circle of vendors orbiting two institutions.
casaContratos.ariaLabel: Vendor capture pattern around SCT/SHCP institutions
casaContratos.findingBody: Seven companies with shared addresses, crossed legal representatives, and a single ultimate beneficial owner concentrated MXN 85B in federal works. In COMPRANET each contract looks legal. Summed, they reveal an institutional capture pattern that no isolated bid could have detected.
casaContratos.footer: Source: COMPRANET · Case 11 (Grupo Higa / Casa Blanca) · approximate network values

insabi.kicker: RUBLI · Seguro Popular vs INSABI · health sector
insabi.headline: The INSABI experiment: four indicators, all worse after 2020
insabi.lede: Direct comparison between the Seguro Popular era (2015–2019) and the INSABI era (2020–2023) in the same health sector. Each indicator is a dot strip: green when Seguro Popular was better, red when INSABI worsened the metric.
insabi.stat1Label: INSABI direct award · vs. ~78% health avg.
insabi.ariaLabel: Seguro Popular vs INSABI comparison chart
insabi.legendBottom: green = healthy metric · red = deteriorated · each dot = 2pp
insabi.findingBody: INSABI ran four years. In all four, every health-system procurement indicator worsened: less competition, more risk, more shortage, greater year-end spend concentration. The institute was dissolved in 2023; the consequences for patients — children without chemo, adults without insulin — were not.
insabi.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2015–2023 · ASF Cuenta Pública 2020–2023 · PAHO UNOPS reports

infraestructura.headline: Public works lost real competition over 14 years
infraestructura.lede: Each bar shows how infrastructure contracts were awarded that year: red = direct award, orange = single-bidder tender, green = open competitive tender. Green blocks shrank from 66% to 32% between 2010 and 2024.
infraestructura.ariaLabel: Infrastructure contracting by year 2010–2024: direct award, single bidder, open tender percentages
infraestructura.findingBody: Over 14 years open competitive tendering in public works dropped from 66% to 32%. Tren Maya, Dos Bocas and AIFA were executed through military entities exempt from normal procurement rules — moving hundreds of billions of pesos outside the competitive framework.
infraestructura.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2010–2024 · infrastructure sector · 196,540 single-bidder contracts

communityBubbles.headline: 22 vendors co-contract with a single public institution
communityBubbles.lede: Each node is a vendor; size is its share of total value; color is its risk tier. High-risk vendors cluster near the hub — a pattern consistent with institutional capture.
communityBubbles.ariaLabel: Network graph of 22 vendors connected to a central institution hub, colored by risk tier
communityBubbles.findingBody: The highest-risk vendors orbit close to the hub — shared addresses, common legal representatives, or co-bidding patterns indicate possible ghost-company networks.
communityBubbles.footer: Source: Louvain community detection · 200K+ vendors · RUBLI v0.8.5

sectorRiskHeatmap.headline: Risk concentrates in Salud and Agricultura during the AMLO sexenio
sectorRiskHeatmap.subline: 12 sectors · 2002–2025 · Cell intensity = avg risk score · 5 administrations
sectorRiskHeatmap.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2002–2025 · RUBLI v0.8.5 · Administration boundaries from INE records

sectorRiskTrends.headline: Agriculture risk doubled after Segalmex — Salud spiked during COVID and stayed elevated
sectorRiskTrends.subline: High-risk rate per sector · 2010–2025 · Toggle sectors to compare
sectorRiskTrends.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2010–2025 · RUBLI v0.8.5 per-sector models · Scandal dates from public record

temporalRisk.headline: COVID-19 drove a 30% spike in procurement risk — and it never fully came back down
temporalRisk.subline: High-risk rate and avg risk score · 2010–2025 · Scandal annotations marked
temporalRisk.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2010–2025 · RUBLI v0.8.5 risk model · Scandal dates from public record

seasonality.headline: Every December, procurement risk spikes 64% — for 23 consecutive years
seasonality.lede: The "December effect" is the single most persistent anomaly in Mexican federal procurement. Government agencies rush to exhaust annual budgets before the fiscal year closes, pushing average contract amounts to MXN 4.87M — nearly double the yearly average of MXN 2.9M.
seasonality.stat2Label: avg contract in December · 1.7× annual avg
seasonality.oecdBody: The OECD recommends even distribution of procurement across the fiscal year. Year-end spending spikes are a recognized corruption risk indicator in all OECD integrity frameworks — Mexico's December pattern is among the most extreme in OECD member states.
seasonality.footer: Source: COMPRANET 2002–2025 · 3.05M contracts · RUBLI v0.8.5 risk scores
